Para trazar la historia geológica de Gomera, fijemos un punto 
nuestra atención en la manera como están distribuidos los mate-
riales cuyo estudio ha sido el objeto del anterior capítulo. La figu-
ra 33 da de ello una idea bastante aproximada. 
El basalto de andenes (p) con sus inseparables las labradori-
tas, ocupa una posición marginal, completamente excluido de las 
regiones elevadas, puesto que en ningún punto de la isla alcanza 
a los I.OOO metros de altitud. Su disposición, relativamente a la 
línea divisoria de aguas, no puede ser más instructiva. 
Tratemos de señalar esta divisoria, operación que, como ya 
hemos indicado, ofrece ciertas dificultades por la especial topo-
grafía de la isla. Puede hacérsela partir de la montaíia Chejeré, nO 
lejos de ia punta de los Órganos (véase el mapa), y se dirige al 
Sur, formando un arco cóncavo hacia Levante, hasta pasar entre 
las cabezas de los barrancos de Macayo y de Valle Gran Rey. 
Desde aquí se dirige por Laguna Grande al punto culminante de 
la isla, el Alto Garajonay. Se incurva luego en ansa convexa 
hacia Alajeró, para buscar los roques de Agando, Ojila y Zarci-
ta, a los que enlaza en línea irregular. Después, por entre las 
cabeceras del barranco de Hermigua y de los que afluyen al de 
la Laja, va a unirse con la cumbre de la Villa o del Carbonero, 
y viene, por fin, a terminar hacia la punta de San Cristóbal. For-
ma, pues, en conjunto un arco que va del Norte al Este, cón-
cavo hacia el NE. 
Toda la vertiente externa de este arco, desde unos 6oo 6 700 
metros de altitud media hasta el mar mismo, está cubierta de
© Del documento, los autores. Digitalización realizada por ULPGC. Biblioteca universitaria, 2010

corrientes basálticas, superpuestas a veces en número verdadera-
mente excepcional y de potencia notable. En la parte interna 
del arco se encuentran dos manchas bastante extensas, la situada

al Norte de San Sebastián y la que podríamos llamar de Agulo, 
por comprender casi todo el término de este Ayuntamiento. 
Estas rocas son las más frescas de la isla, generalmente muy
Trab. del Mus. Xac. de Cieñe. Nat. de Madrid. —Serie Geol., núm. 23. —1918.
© Del documento, los autores. Digitalización realizada por ULPGC. Biblioteca universitaria, 2010

compactas, sin que entre ellas se interpongan materiales sueltos 
ni tobas en cantidad considerable. 
Junto al borde más elevado de este festón basáltico se locali­
zan los escasos residuos de explosión (C) que se encuentran en la 
isla, especialmente hacia Laguna Grande, donde hay una comar­
ca de topografía volcánica moderna y hasta el único cráter que 
probablemente se conserva en la isla.

Los basaltos duros y pesados, a que en Vallehermoso llaman 
«roca viva», y que, como ya hemos dicho, no se encuentran más 
que sueltos en los barrancos, denotan que la mancha de Agulo 
se extendió más hacia Poniente, y que una erosión poderosa, 
que no puede menos de haberse proseguido durante bastante 
tiempo, ha destruido una buena parte del escudo basáltico.

El complejo de rocas traquifonolíticas (T) sobre que se exten­
dieron las corrientes básicas modernas es de una estructura más 
difícil de descifrar, sobre todo por el tránsito insensible que hay 
entre las traquitas propiamente dichas y las interesantes fonoli­
tas de egirina, con las derivaciones hacia traquiandesitas y aun 
sanidinitas. Y queda la duda de si todos estos materiales corres­
ponden a un mismo período eruptivo, o si hubo en realidad dos 
períodos, uno traquítico y otro fonolítico.

A esta última opinión nos inclinamos más bien, teniendo en 
cuenta, sobre todo, que las fonolitas propiamente dichas forman 
siempre roques y fortalezas que surgen a través de las traquitas 
y de las traquifonolitas. Estas últimas se van haciendo más fuer­
tes y uniformes cuanto más profundas, como puede observarse, 
por ejemplo, siguiendo hasta el mar el barranco de Valleher­
moso y viendo la composición petrográfica que se va encon­
trando. Las traquitas puras son el tipo menos abundante del 
grupo, y aunque sus elementos suelen conservarse frescos, la 
roca se encuentra en un estado de fácil disgregación que indica, 
por lo menos, el efecto de grandes presiones.

Las rocas tobáceas y cavernosas de esta época forman casi 
siempre riscos o picos desgarrados, que se destacan en el paisaje 
como testigos de capas más extensas destruidas por la erosión. 
Frecuentemente están recubiertas por las corrientes basálticas y 
en inmediato contacto con ellas.
© Del documento, los autores. Digitalización realizada por ULPGC. Biblioteca universitaria, 2010

Resulta, pues, claramente la existencia de dos épocas erupti-
vas bien separadas, la basáltica y la traquifonolítica, cuyos mate-
riales respectivos se encuentran en la relación que indica el corte 
esquemático de la figura 34. No está tan evidente que el período 
de emisiones no básicas pueda ser dividido en otros dos, uno 
más antiguo, de materiales ácidos (traquitas), y otro de rocas 
neutras (fonolitas). En todo caso, estas últimas debieron sufrir 
una acción erosiva enorme, que ha logrado dejar al descubierto 
en muchas partes el material traquítico, y que sólo ha permitido 
conservarse a la fonolita que rellenó los diques y chimeneas de 
salida, constituyendo hoy roques y fortalezas. 
Nos queda, por último, considerar el papel de ciertas rocas 
poco frecuentes, pero de gran interés teórico, que son las que 
hemos llamado rocas de substrátum. Son principalmente las dia-
basas, que se encuentran en los barrancos de la región de Agulo, 
pero no in situ, sino sueltas y en pequeños fragmentos. Se trata, 
sin duda, de materiales arrancados por las primeras erupciones a 
una base plutónica anteterciaria, en que se apoya todo el edificio 
eruptivo posterior. Son, pues, verdaderos enclaves, aunque en los 
ejemplares pornosotros recogidos no se conserve la roca en volven. 
te y no se pueda, por lo tanto, decidir si son o no homeógenos-
En la base del barranco de Piedra Gorda, en Agulo, y for-
mando un pequeño dique, hemos encontrado una verdadera 
andesita de caracteres bastante arcaicos. También en la parte 
profunda del barranco de Hermigua hemos hallado in situ una 
traquiandesita bastante alterada. Estos hallazgos pudieran ser 
indicio de una erupción de tipo andesítico, acaso inmediatamente 
apoyada en el substrátum holocristalino, y anterior, por lo tanto, 
al período eruptivo traquifonolítico. La rareza de estas rocas no 
permite hacer afirmaciones concretas en este respecto.

Resumiendo todos estos hechos, he aquí las etapas sucesivas 
que en la historia de Gomera nos permite reconocer su actual 
estado : 
l.° 
Existencia de un substrátum plutónico de rocas holocris-

Trab. del Mus. Nac. de Cieñe. Nat. de Madrid.—Serie Geol., nám. 23. — 1918. 
G
© Del documento, los autores. Digitalización realizada por ULPGC. Biblioteca universitaria, 2010
© Del documento, los autores. Digitalización realizada por ULPGC. Biblioteca universitaria, 2010

talinas (diabasas), sobre el que se ha desarrollado todo el edificio 
eruptivo moderno. No conocemos la profundidad a que se en-
cuentran estos materiales, ni, por consiguiente, la verdadera forma 
que afectan; muy verosímilmente constituirán un batolito.

Puede sospecharse la existencia de una erupción andesítica 
inmediatamente superpuesta a estas rocas de la base; pero no hay 
datos suficientes para afirmarlo de una manera definitiva. 
2." 
Período eruptivo de rocas traquifonolíticas, muy dura-
dero e importante. A él se debe principalmente la formación de 
la isla. Seguramente las rocas acidas (traquitas) emitidas al prin-
cipio del período fueron sucesivamente perdiendo este carácter 
(traquifonolitas intermedias) hasta devenir neutras (fonolitas). 
3.° 
Después de la emisión de este grupo de rocas transcurre 
un largo período de calma, durante el cual la isla es fuertemente 
erosionada, y las rocas formadas de materiales explosivos (bre-
chas y tobas de presión principalmente) desaparecen en gran 
parte. Los restos que de ellas persisten bastan, sin embargo, para 
demostrar el carácter eminentemente explosivo y violento de las 
erupciones, lo que, por otra parte, está de perfecto acuerdo con 
el carácter químico de estas rocas.

4° 
Emisión, en forma efusiva más bien que explosiva, de 
rocas básicas (basaltos y labradoritas) en cantidad importante, 
pero en modo alguno comparable con el volumen de las rocas 
acidas y neutras. Las bocas de erupción debieron ser laterales 
con relación al edificio ya entonces constituido, y situadas prin-
cipalmente en sus laderas meridional y occidental. 
5.° 
Nuevo período de tranquilidad, en el que actualmente 
nos encontramos y durante el cual han desaparecido casi total-
mente los aparatos de emisión y aun una buena parte de las co-
rrientes lávicas. 
Un hecho interesante que este proceso reseñado hace resaltar 
es el tránsito sucesivo desde las erupciones más acidas a las más 
básicas, a medida que el tiempo ha ido transcurriendo. Es el 
proceso clásico, que no siempre ha podido reconocerse, sin em-
bargo, pero del que la isla de Gomera se puede poner como 
ejemplo insuperable.
La edad absoluta de las erupciones que constituyeron este
Trab. del Mus. Nac. de Cieñe. Nat. de Madrid. — Serie Geol., DÚm. 23.— igi8.
© Del documento, los autores. Digitalización realizada por ULPGC. Biblioteca universitaria, 2010

macizo volcánico es hasta ahora imposible de fijar, por falta de 
materiales sedimentarios a que referir los períodos de emisión. 
Por lo que se sabe respecto a las demás islas del archipiélago 
canario y por lo que permiten sospechar los datos que nuestros 
estudios nos han proporcionado, puede proponerse como muy 
probable la cronología siguiente : el substrátum holocrislalino 
será seguramente anteterciario, pertenecerán a la era cenozoica 
las erupciones del grupo traquifonolítico, y serán cuaternarias o 
acaso pliocenas las emisiones del grupo basáltico.

Insistamos en lo largo del período de tranquilidad que sin 
duda ha seguido a las erupciones basálticas, comprobado por la 
desaparición de los conos volcánicos y por el valor de las accio-
nes erosivas que han hendido la isla con barrancos de profundi-
dad extraordinaria. Este carácter resalta si se compara Gomera 
con las islas próximas, no sólo con La Palma y Tenerife, en que 
los volcanes son todavía activos, sino con Hierro, que no habien-
do sufrido erupciones en la época histórica, ni conservando 
siquiera fenómenos fumarolianos, está, sin embargo, cubierta en 
gran parte de volcanes en perfecto estado de conservación. 
La isla de Gomera es, sin duda, la más estable, la de más 
antiguo consolidada de las Canarias, al menos de las occidenta-
les, que son las que conocemos con detalle. Ni siquiera hay en 
ella pruebas decisivas de movimientos importantes en la vertical, 
ni playas levantadas que acusen una emersión, ni un reborde con-
tinental bien marcado: cantiles imponentes, como corresponde a 
la acción erosiva de un fuerte mar sobre rocas de esta naturaleza, 
y sondas uniformemente crecientes a partir de la misma orilla. 
Buscando analogía de constitución entre esta isla y las demás 
Canarias, sólo la encontramos, entre lo que conocemos, con la 
parte más antigua de Tenerife, con los materiales que por debajo 
de su corteza de rocas eruptivas modernas aparecen en las regio-
nes de Anaga, Teño y Adeje. Precisamente la porción de Go-
mera que hace frente a Tenerife, la comprendida de Valleher-
© Del documento, los autores. Digitalización realizada por ULPGC. Biblioteca universitaria, 2010

moso a San Sebastián, es la más rica en rocas traquií'onolíticas 
y la de topografía más semejante a la de Teño. Las cumbres de 
Gala, en Tenerife, y la cumbre de la Villa, que las hace frente 
en Gomera, al otro lado del canal que separa ambas islas, son 
idénticas en forma y naturaleza, como partes separadas de una 
misma unidad. 
También hemos visto que es análoga en las dos islas vecinas 
la disposición del talud costero, como lo demuestra la situación 
del veril de las lOO brazas (véase pág. 17). 
Y aunque la profundidad del canal Gomera-Tenerife es muy 
considerable (1.550 brazas), y mayor que el Gomera-La Palma 
(1.390 brazas), damos poco valor a este dato, por tratarse de 
regiones eruptivas en que siempre son frecuentes los grandes 
desniveles y la fuerte accidentación. Entre Hierro y Gomera la 
sonda registra profundidades de 1.600 brazas.

En resumen, creemos que se puede considerar a la isla de 
Gomera como una porción del substrátum terciario de Tenerife, 
como una dependencia de esta isla, aunque separadas ambas por 
la depresión estrecha y profunda del canal marino que entre 
ellas se tiende.

Para concluir nuestro trabajo, digamos todavía algunas pala-
bras acerca del problema del agua, tan importante en Gomera y 
en todo el archipiélago. 
La abundancia relativa de aguas en esta isla se relaciona con 
aquella clara estructura que hemos descrito de basaltos porosos 
apoyados en un plano inclinado de rocas antiguas menos per-
meables, tanto por su disposición en masa como por la frecuente 
acillificación de sus materiales. Esto mismo explica que las aguas 
sean más abundantes y surjan en mejores condiciones de apro-
vechamiento (a mayor altura) en los barrancos de la banda norte 
que en los del Sur. Éstos suelen estar total o casi totalmente 
abiertos en el basalto, mientras que aquéllos llegan a hendir 
pronto el conjunto de rocas traquifonolíticas, y el nivel acuífero 
principal de la isla aflora en la parte alta de las laderas.
Trab. del Mus. Nac. de Cienc. Nat. de Madrid.—Serie Geol., núm, 23. —1918,
© Del documento, los autores. Digitalización realizada por ULPGC. Biblioteca universitaria, 2010

Rl problema del aprovechamiento es distinto, por consi­
guiente, en ima y otra banda. En el Norte será, sobre todo, 
cuestión de mejorar las captaciones de los manantiales existen­
tes, más que de investigación de otros posibles. Estas mejoras 
han de consistir, principalmente, en la perforación de galerías con­
venientemente orientadas, que, a partir de los actuales surgentes, 
vayan subiendo de nivel por el plano de separación de las dos 
formaciones eruptivas. No es problema sencillo la dirección de 
estos trabajos, que mal concebidos pueden ser contraproducen­
tes. En materiales tan heterogéneos y tan irregularmente dis­
puestos como lo son siempre los volcánicos, es muy fácil abrir 
a las aguas un camino por donde pasen a niveles inferiores per­
meables y se pierdan total o parcialmente.

En la banda sur el nivel acuífero rico aflora en muchos pun­
tos por debajo del nivel del mar, o por lo menos en la parte más 
baja de los barrancos, donde los materiales sueltos ocultan la 
corriente, que será casi siempre subálvea. La perforación de ga­
lerías penetrantes que vayan a buscar en su trayecto subterráneo 
al nivel acuífero que sin duda existe, es una operación perfec­
tamente posible, pero siempre aventurada. Los estudios previo;; 
más minuciosos y mejor orientados" no permitirán nunca garan­
tizar a estas obras contra un probable fracaso.

En resumen, lo que puede hacerse para mejorar las condicio­
nes hidrológicas de ésta como de las demás islas debe ser, sobr<> 
todo, conservar lo existente y procurar acrecer por medios indi­
rectos, lentos acaso, pero sin duda eficaces, el volumen de agua 
disponible. Nos referimos con esto principalmente a la conser­
vación y mejora de los montes, que por su fortuna posee Go­
mera en cantidad relativamente superior a las demás islas. 
Es un hecho, comprobado universalmente, que la desapari­
ción de las grandes masas de vegetación disminuye o anula el ren­
dimiento de los manantiales en las proximidades de la comarca 
talada, y hace más irregular el régimen de las corrientes superfi­
ciales. En (lOmera como por todas partes, los bosques son ata­
cados sin piedad, y si no se les defiende y protege con energía, 
los intereses agrícolas de la isla se resentirán en plazo breve. 
La acción de los árboles condensando las nieblas es, sin duda.
© Del documento, los autores. Digitalización realizada por ULPGC. Biblioteca universitaria, 2010

OBSERVACIONES 
GE0LÓG;CAS EN LA ISI.A DE COMERÁ 
87

muy importante para la alimentación de las corrientes subterrá-
neas. Nosotros hemos visto en los bosques gomeros cómo a los 
pocos momentos de entrar en ellos una nube, el agua escurría 
por todas partes y formaba charcos y arroyitos, rápidamente 
absorbidos por aquel suelo eminentemente poroso. 
Si este dato de visu no bastara, recuérdense las experiencias 
realizadas cerca de la Ciudad del Cabo por Marloth con sus dos 
pluviómetros comparadores, uno de la forma corriente y otro re-
cubierto por una veintena de cañitas, reunidas en sus extremos 
por una red metálica. En aquél, que no recogía más que el agua 
de lluvia, se obtuvieron en dos meses 125 milímetros de agua, 
mientras que en éste, que condensaba las nieblas, se obtuvieron 
en el mismo tiempo 2 metros. Este resultado no debe extrañar 
en Canarias, donde es bien conocida la tradición del famoso 
árbol del agua, el garoé, de la isla de Hierro.

A estos efectos condensadores debe agregarse el de lenti-
ficar extraordinariamente la evaporación superficial, que ejercen 
las copas de los árboles. .Según las experiencias de la Escuela 
Forestal de Nancy, se evapora tres veces más agua en la tierra 
desnuda que bajo la pantalla formada por el follaje del bos-
que. Y aunque es verdad que esta pantalla detiene también par-
te de la precipitación atmosférica, es en grado mucho menor, 
puesto que, según las observaciones de la Escuela citada, oscila 
entre un 8 y un 17 por 100 del agua caída.

A la acción de los bosques podría ayudarse en cierta medi-
da con la creación de aljibes, en la forma que hace tiempo reco-
mendamos para la isla de Hierro, y por la constitución de depó-
sitos accidentales en las cabeceras de los barrancos, trabajos nada 
difíciles ni costosos. Con ellos se lograría un máximo aprovecha-
miento de las aguas pluviales, poco abundantes en Gomera.

production from the Devonian lime has been unsatisiactory in Indiana be-
cause of the non-permeable character of the reservoir rock. The only im-
portant production from this horizon in the region is in the "Yiartinsville 
pool in Clark County, Illinois, about 25 miles west. The oil discovered in 
the Indiana field is of 47° gravity and yields 56 per cl'nt gasoline on 
straight distillation. 
A second pool of importance has been developed near the town of 
Francisco, and about 6 miles east of Princeton, in Gibson County, Indi-
ana. Mann and Huber of Evansville, Indiana, following the recommenda-
tions of Gail F. Moulton in a private report, drilled a new test north of 
some older small wells, and found I 50 barrels initial production. Since 
that time, three other wells have been completed, the best of which pro-
duced 150 barrels per day, on an average, for the ftrst two months after 
being put on the pump. 
The production in the Francisco pool is obtained from a Chester sane!, 
which, according to the correlations of the Indiana Geological Survey, is 
the Sample sand. This sand is reached at a depth of r ,400 feet, and pro-
duces a heavy oil of about 28° A.P.I. gnn·i1y.

The oil sand is very open and continuous i11 this lield, a rondition not 
generally found in this part of the country. The fold on which the pro-
duction is obtained is likewise somewhat more pronounced than any 
others which have yet been described for the general region. The principal 
operating difficulties are to keep from ruining the wells by drilling too 
deep into water, and to separate the oil when water is produced with it. 
Four new wells are now producing, and four more drilling. The pres-
ent production of this pool is more than 300 )Jarrcls, <Jr mon· than une-
eighth of the total for the state of Indiana. 
The Vigo-Sullivan County pool together with the Francisco pool are 
principally responsible for the increase in production in Indiana this year.

SLOPE OF THE VENEZUELAN A:\IDES' 
Dr. N. H. Darton, of the l:nited States Geological Survey, recently 
collected on the east slope of the Venezuelan Andes specimens of a dark-
colored partly crystalline limestone that carries Foraminifera of Eocene

age. This material was collected half a mile southeast of the village of 
Masparito, or about 12 miles east of Calderas, in the state of Zamora (U. 
S. Geol. Survey locality l/n89). On Jahn's geologic map of Venezuela1

this locality would fall almost due north of Barinas near the eastern edge 
of the area mapped as schist and gneiss. This Eocene limestone is a thin 
bed lying near the base of a thick series of shales overlying a quartzite. 
Additional float material was collected on Rio Arana, west of Calderas. 
The collection of float limestone also contains specimens of a dark-colored 
Carboniferous Productus-bearing limestone that in gross features is sur-
prisingly similar to the Eocene limestone. So far as known, this record, 
which is made available through the kindness of Dr. A. C. Veatch, is the 
first one of Eocene deposits in the drainage basin of the Orinoco. The 
float material also establishes the presence of Carboniferous limestone in 
the Venezuelan Andes. Heretofore, Carboniferous beds have not been re-
ported north of Peru and the Amazon Valley in eastern Brazil. 
The Foraminifera include a "Nummulites," a stellatc ''Orthophrag-
mina," and a Lepidocyclina. The megalospheric form of the Lepidocy-
clina belongs to the subgenus Pliolepidina and represents L. panamensis 
Cushman• or a very similar species. Vaughan3 suggests that L. pana-
mensis is a synonym of Pliolepidina tobleri Douville,4 which is based on 
material from Trinidad. Douville has never published figures showing the 
shape and exterior of tobleri and, therefore, it is not entirely recognizable. 
L. tobleri is the monotype of Pliolepidina Douville. When this name was 
first proposed in 1915 "Pliolepidina sp." was the only species assigned to 
it. In DouvillC's 1917 paper there is no indication that Pliolepidina tobleri 
is the "Pliolepidina sp." of 1915. Strictly speaking, the status of Pliole-
pidina was not fixed until the 1924 synonymy was published. 
The Venezuelan Lepidocyclina, like panamensis and tobleri, has an un-
usually large nucleoconch, or embryonic shell, which in vertical sections 
appears rectangular and is composed of one or several chambers, depend-
ing on the plane of the section, one of which generally is extraordinarily 
large. A microspheric form, assumed to represent the same species, is 
more abundant and larger. Its test is very thick, and many lateral cham-

bers are piled one on top of the other in vertical series. In both forms 
rather slender pillars, which may not reach the surface, lie between the 
lateral chambers. 
Precisely the same couple of megalospheric and microspheric forms, 
both of which are very striking, are found together in the Caribbean 
coastal region of Colombia in beds that are considered of middle Eocene 
age. H. Hodson' records similar macrospheric forms from an unnamed 
locality in the Maracaibo basin. Tobler2 has described a remarkably sim-
ilar couple (Lepidocyclina [Pliol~pidina?] luxurians Tobler) from southern 
Sumatra. This species is very similar to specimens from Venezuela and 
Colombia, but the pillars are less developed. Douville, and H. Hodson 
following Douville's suggestion, thought that these specimens with rela-
tively enormous nucleoconchs might be monstrosities, hut, as they have 
now been found on the Pacific coast of Panama, at many places in Colom-
bia, in the Maracaibo basin, on the east slope of the Venezuelan Andes, in 
Trinidad, and in Sumatra, it is safe to assume that the development of 
these unusual nucleoconchs is a normal character. Many of the Colom-
bian specimens on which the peripheral flange is broken off have a nucleo-
conch that is almost as wide as the entire umhonal part 1>f the test. 
The type material of L. panamensis is represented by Figure 6 of 
Plate 39 in Bulletin 103 of the United States :'\ational Museum. It was 
collected near the mouth of Rio Tonosi, Los Santos Province, on the Pa-

production from the Devonian lime has been unsatisiactory in Indiana be-
cause of the non-permeable character of the reservoir rock. The only im-
portant production from this horizon in the region is in the "Yiartinsville 
pool in Clark County, Illinois, about 25 miles west. The oil discovered in 
the Indiana field is of 47° gravity and yields 56 per cl'nt gasoline on 
straight distillation. 
A second pool of importance has been developed near the town of 
Francisco, and about 6 miles east of Princeton, in Gibson County, Indi-
ana. Mann and Huber of Evansville, Indiana, following the recommenda-
tions of Gail F. Moulton in a private report, drilled a new test north of 
some older small wells, and found I 50 barrels initial production. Since 
that time, three other wells have been completed, the best of which pro-
duced 150 barrels per day, on an average, for the ftrst two months after 
being put on the pump. 
The production in the Francisco pool is obtained from a Chester sane!, 
which, according to the correlations of the Indiana Geological Survey, is 
the Sample sand. This sand is reached at a depth of r ,400 feet, and pro-
duces a heavy oil of about 28° A.P.I. gnn·i1y.

The oil sand is very open and continuous i11 this lield, a rondition not 
generally found in this part of the country. The fold on which the pro-
duction is obtained is likewise somewhat more pronounced than any 
others which have yet been described for the general region. The principal 
operating difficulties are to keep from ruining the wells by drilling too 
deep into water, and to separate the oil when water is produced with it. 
Four new wells are now producing, and four more drilling. The pres-
ent production of this pool is more than 300 )Jarrcls, <Jr mon· than une-
eighth of the total for the state of Indiana. 
The Vigo-Sullivan County pool together with the Francisco pool are 
principally responsible for the increase in production in Indiana this year.

SLOPE OF THE VENEZUELAN A:\IDES' 
Dr. N. H. Darton, of the l:nited States Geological Survey, recently 
collected on the east slope of the Venezuelan Andes specimens of a dark-
colored partly crystalline limestone that carries Foraminifera of Eocene

'Published with the permission of the director, C. S. Ceological Sun-ey, and of the 
director, exploration department, Sinclair Consolidated Oil Corpora1iun.

Downloaded from https://pubs.geoscienceworld.org/aapgbull/article-pdf/11/9/992/4350327/aapg_1927_0011_0009_0992.pdf
by California Inst of Technology  user

age. This material was collected half a mile southeast of the village of 
Masparito, or about 12 miles east of Calderas, in the state of Zamora (U. 
S. Geol. Survey locality l/n89). On Jahn's geologic map of Venezuela1

this locality would fall almost due north of Barinas near the eastern edge 
of the area mapped as schist and gneiss. This Eocene limestone is a thin 
bed lying near the base of a thick series of shales overlying a quartzite. 
Additional float material was collected on Rio Arana, west of Calderas. 
The collection of float limestone also contains specimens of a dark-colored 
Carboniferous Productus-bearing limestone that in gross features is sur-
prisingly similar to the Eocene limestone. So far as known, this record, 
which is made available through the kindness of Dr. A. C. Veatch, is the 
first one of Eocene deposits in the drainage basin of the Orinoco. The 
float material also establishes the presence of Carboniferous limestone in 
the Venezuelan Andes. Heretofore, Carboniferous beds have not been re-
ported north of Peru and the Amazon Valley in eastern Brazil. 
The Foraminifera include a "Nummulites," a stellatc ''Orthophrag-
mina," and a Lepidocyclina. The megalospheric form of the Lepidocy-
clina belongs to the subgenus Pliolepidina and represents L. panamensis 
Cushman• or a very similar species. Vaughan3 suggests that L. pana-
mensis is a synonym of Pliolepidina tobleri Douville,4 which is based on 
material from Trinidad. Douville has never published figures showing the 
shape and exterior of tobleri and, therefore, it is not entirely recognizable. 
L. tobleri is the monotype of Pliolepidina Douville. When this name was 
first proposed in 1915 "Pliolepidina sp." was the only species assigned to 
it. In DouvillC's 1917 paper there is no indication that Pliolepidina tobleri 
is the "Pliolepidina sp." of 1915. Strictly speaking, the status of Pliole-
pidina was not fixed until the 1924 synonymy was published. 
The Venezuelan Lepidocyclina, like panamensis and tobleri, has an un-
usually large nucleoconch, or embryonic shell, which in vertical sections 
appears rectangular and is composed of one or several chambers, depend-
ing on the plane of the section, one of which generally is extraordinarily 
large. A microspheric form, assumed to represent the same species, is 
more abundant and larger. Its test is very thick, and many lateral cham-

L. duplicata Cushman,4 which is found in the upper Eocene limestone 
of Chiriqui Province on the Pacific coast of Panama, also is a Plivlepidina; 
at all events the macrospheric form identified by Vaughan as represent-
ing du.-plicata is a Plivlepidina. This species instead of panamensis may be 
a synonym of tobleri. So far as the development of pillars is concerned, the 
macrospheric form of duplicata resembles the East Indian lu.rnrians. L. 
duplicata, the type of which is a microspheric form, is the type by original 
designation of Afulticyclina Cushman.5 Inasmuch as the development of 
several layers of equatorial cells toward the periphery, which was regarded 
as the characteristic feature of lvlulticydina, is found in many species, this 
name has been disregarded. lvlidticydina, based on a microspheric form, 
seems to be a synonym of Pliolepidina, which was based on a megalospher-
ic form. It is apparent that under different conditions this situation could 
be a fruitful source for nomenclatorial dispute. 
In tropical America species of Pliolepidiu.a, similar to the one collected 
on the east slope of the Venezuelan Andes, arc found in both middle and 
upper Eocene deposits. The Point Bon tour beds on the island oi Trinidad 
that carry L. tobleri are of upper Eocene age,'' though they were first called 
"Stampian" by Douville. They also carry a stcllatc ''Orthvphragmina," 
Cisseis asteriscus Guppy, a group that so far as known occurs only in upper 
Eocene deposits in America, but in Europe it also is found in the middle 
Eocene. Stellate "Orthophragminas" have been found in the tloat material

production from the Devonian lime has been unsatisiactory in Indiana be-

pool in Clark County, Illinois, about 25 miles west. The oil discovered in 
the Indiana field is of 47° gravity and yields 56 per cl'nt gasoline on 
straight distillation. 
A second pool of importance has been developed near the town of 
Francisco, and about 6 miles east of Princeton, in Gibson County, Indi-
ana. Mann and Huber of Evansville, Indiana, following the recommenda-
tions of Gail F. Moulton in a private report, drilled a new test north of 
some older small wells, and found I 50 barrels initial production. Since 
that time, three other wells have been completed, the best of which pro-
duced 150 barrels per day, on an average, for the ftrst two months after 
being put on the pump. 
The production in the Francisco pool is obtained from a Chester sane!, 
which, according to the correlations of the Indiana Geological Survey, is 
the Sample sand. This sand is reached at a depth of r ,400 feet, and pro-
duces a heavy oil of about 28° A.P.I. gnn·i1y.

The oil sand is very open and continuous i11 this lield, a rondition not 
generally found in this part of the country. The fold on which the pro-
duction is obtained is likewise somewhat more pronounced than any 
others which have yet been described for the general region. The principal 
operating difficulties are to keep from ruining the wells by drilling too 
deep into water, and to separate the oil when water is produced with it. 
Four new wells are now producing, and four more drilling. The pres-
ent production of this pool is more than 300 )Jarrcls, <Jr mon· than une-
eighth of the total for the state of Indiana. 
The Vigo-Sullivan County pool together with the Francisco pool are 
principally responsible for the increase in production in Indiana this year.

age. This material was collected half a mile southeast of the village of 
Masparito, or about 12 miles east of Calderas, in the state of Zamora (U. 
S. Geol. Survey locality l/n89). On Jahn's geologic map of Venezuela1

this locality would fall almost due north of Barinas near the eastern edge 
of the area mapped as schist and gneiss. This Eocene limestone is a thin 
bed lying near the base of a thick series of shales overlying a quartzite. 
Additional float material was collected on Rio Arana, west of Calderas. 
The collection of float limestone also contains specimens of a dark-colored 
Carboniferous Productus-bearing limestone that in gross features is sur-
prisingly similar to the Eocene limestone. So far as known, this record, 
which is made available through the kindness of Dr. A. C. Veatch, is the 
first one of Eocene deposits in the drainage basin of the Orinoco. The 
float material also establishes the presence of Carboniferous limestone in 
the Venezuelan Andes. Heretofore, Carboniferous beds have not been re-
ported north of Peru and the Amazon Valley in eastern Brazil. 
The Foraminifera include a "Nummulites," a stellatc ''Orthophrag-
mina," and a Lepidocyclina. The megalospheric form of the Lepidocy-
clina belongs to the subgenus Pliolepidina and represents L. panamensis 
Cushman• or a very similar species. Vaughan3 suggests that L. pana-
mensis is a synonym of Pliolepidina tobleri Douville,4 which is based on 
material from Trinidad. Douville has never published figures showing the 
shape and exterior of tobleri and, therefore, it is not entirely recognizable. 
L. tobleri is the monotype of Pliolepidina Douville. When this name was 
first proposed in 1915 "Pliolepidina sp." was the only species assigned to 
it. In DouvillC's 1917 paper there is no indication that Pliolepidina tobleri 
is the "Pliolepidina sp." of 1915. Strictly speaking, the status of Pliole-
pidina was not fixed until the 1924 synonymy was published. 
The Venezuelan Lepidocyclina, like panamensis and tobleri, has an un-
usually large nucleoconch, or embryonic shell, which in vertical sections 
appears rectangular and is composed of one or several chambers, depend-
ing on the plane of the section, one of which generally is extraordinarily 
large. A microspheric form, assumed to represent the same species, is 
more abundant and larger. Its test is very thick, and many lateral cham-

The International Accountants Society, Inc., is recognized as pre­
eminent among nonresident accounting schools. The following 
seven points of superiority explain why.

1. The International Accountant* Society, Inc. (established in 1903) 
is the oldest home-study school of accounting.

2. The I. A. S. confines its instruction efforts exclusively to the sub­
ject of accounting, including its various branches. No attempt is 
made to cover the general educational field. By specializing in 
one subject the I. A. S. believes the subject can be treated far 
more intelligently and effectively than it can be done by an insti­
tution which scatters its efforts over a wide variety of subjects.

3. The I. A. S. numbers more Certified Public Accountants among its 
students and graduates than can be claimed by any other similar 
institution.

4. The I. A. S. has the largest Active Faculty of Certified Public 
Accountants connected with any educational institution in the 
United States.

5. The I. A. S. guarantees that all students’ examination papers are 
examined and graded by Certified Public Accountants.

6. The I. A. S. has the most elastic and extensive elective system 
of instruction offered by any home-study school of accounting. 
The course is so arranged that the student can select a large por­
tion of his lessons to cover subjects pertaining directly to work 
in which he is especially interested, such as that of the Public 
Accountant, the Cost Accountant, the General Executive, etc.

7. The I. A. S. Course is most interesting. Each lesson is a separate 
unit and is prepared in loose-leaf form. This permits of constant 
revision so that the course is kept up to date at all times.

The foregoing seven points are strong features which a good method 
of instruction should possess. Those considering I. A. S. instruction 
for the first time can obtain full information about all these features 
by asking for a copy of our latest catalog, “How to Learn Account­
ing,” which will be forwarded without charge or obligation. Ad­

ACCOUNTANTS' AND BUSINESS EXECUTIVES GENERALLY.

Vol. III
NOVEMBER, 1922
No. 1
Fundamentals of the Business 
Budget
Vice President, International Accountants Society, Inc.

The modern business budget is an 
inheritance from the municipal and 
governmental budget. From the mu­
nicipal and government viewpoint the 
factor of control is all important, the 
importance of economy and efficiency 
often being relatively neglected. This 
is a natural result from the fact that 
governmental units are not judged 
by their profit-making capacity.

The inheritance by business of the 
budget idea requires first of all an 
open-minded appreciation of the fun­
damental differences in purpose of 
the governmental budget as compared 
with the business budget and the con­
sequent vital changes in budgeting 
technic.

The chief characteristic of the gov­
ernmental budget is that its funda­
mental purpose is the control of ex­
penditures, ostensibly in order that 
they may not exceed estimated income 
from taxes and miscellaneous sources, 
but more often in order that a given 
income will be so divided as to insure 
the greatest political effect.

The business budget on the other 
hand is designed solely to control 
business activities for the purpose of 
greater profit.

Here we find the starting point of 
a wide divergence in method.

We find that in the governmental 
budget each activity, branch, or de­
partment of government can be con­
sidered separately, whereas a com­
plex inter-relationship of variables 
characterizes the business budget.

In reality it appears improper to 
speak of a business budget in the sin­
gular. It consists of two separate and 
distinct budgets, constructed for dif­
ferent purposes and by different pro­
cedure. These two business budgets 
are:

1. The profit budget.
2. The financial budget.
The determination of a profit 
budget involves the more or less tech­
nical examination of the following 
three divisions:

1. Sales.
2. Production.
3. Commercial expense.
The construction of each of these 
three subsidiary budgets is again 
based upon distinctive technic and 
procedure. Furthermore, it is inter­
esting to note that they must be 
studied, analyzed, and built in the se­
quence indicated; i. e., the sales budget 
must first be determined, then the pro-

duction budget, and finally the com­
mercial expense budget.

Time limitations prevent giving 
more than a bird’s-eye view to each 
of these three elements of the busi­
ness profit budget.

It is a fundamental point in con­
nection with the sales budget that it 
properly results in building the final 
figures from a separate study of the 
individual sales units. In other 
words, in its simplest phase the final 
figures of expected sales involve a 
study of the characteristics of each 
individual salesman and the probable 
circumstances surrounding his work 
for the coming year. The detail from 
which such a sales budget is con­
structed must be preserved in order 
to account for variations in actual 
sales from those budgeted.

“Based on Optimism”
This may seem to be so obvious as 
to require no comment, and yet we are 
strongly inclined to suspect that the 
average sales budget is based upon 
optimism and the careless manipula­
tion of hoped-for sales percentage in­
creases in total rather than upon a 
thorough analytical study of each one 
of the sales factors of the business.

The building of such a sales budget 
is not, strictly speaking, an account­
ing function. It requires, to be sure, 
a certain knowledge of statistical 
method, but even more it requires a 
broad thorough knowledge of the 
sales function and sales situation.

The production budget cannot be 
constructed until after the sales 
budget has been determined, not only- 
in a yearly total but upon a monthly 
basis. Once done, the production 
budget naturally follows, the proce­
dure required involving a process of 
“backward figuring,” starting with 
finished stocks necessary to fill sales 
requirements and working backward 
by the use of established standards to

determine the quantity of labor, the 
quantity of materials and supplies, 
and the amount of overhead which 
must be planned for.

Construction of a commercial ex­
pense budget is not possible until both 
the sales and the production budgets 
have been finished since the amount 
of such commercial expense will de­
pend to some extent on the kind and 
degree of activity required under the 
first two budgets.

The Most Satisfactory Plan
Probably the most satisfactory plan 
is to approach the commercial expense 
budget with a clear understanding 
that the various expense elements 
must each be considered separately, 
each having some distinct and normal 
relationship to figures contained in 
the sales budget or the production 
budget.

Since these ratios of variability dif­
fer, it becomes necessary to rely 
largely upon past history as reflected 
in the accounts and statistics of pre­
vious years. This is almost entirely 
an interpretive accounting function 
and involves a thorough understand­
ing of statistical method in order to 
determine ratios of variation prop­
erly.

After sales, production, and com­
mercial expense budgets have been 
completed, comprising as they do the 
entire business profit budget, then 
next in order follows the financial 
budget. Quite obviously the problem 
of making a profit for a business and 
the problem of financing that business 
during the profit-making period are 
two distinct problems.

The principal features in construct­
ing a financial budget have to do with 
the methods of estimating the collecti­
bility of accounts receivable and cur­
rent billing and of estimating from 
the purchasing schedules the amounts 
of estimated liabilities, when they are

likely to appear upon the books and 
become due for payment. There are, 
of course, numerous other factors en­
tering into the financial budget.

To summarize, in closing it appears 
that the following points are funda­
mental to any effective use of business 
budgets:

1. An understanding of the differ­
ence in purpose between the business 
budget and the government budget.

2. An understanding of the differ­
ence in purpose between the profit 
budget and the governmental budget.

3. An appreciation of the fact that 
a sales budget can be accurately con­
structed only from a study of each of 
the smallest units contributing to 
sales.

4. Ari understanding of the fact 
that in business budget making the 
sequence of the steps in attacking the 
problem is of fundamental impor­
tance.

The reluctance of business men 
generally to adopt the business budget 
is probably partly due to the inheri­
tance of its name from the govern­
mental field. The very word “budg­
et” carries to some minds an implica­
tion of inaccuracy, guesswork, and 
extravagance. If some new name 
could be devised for the business 
budget which would indicate that it 
represented nothing more than a plan 
—a logical financial plan, it probably 
would eliminate a great deal of execu­
tive opposition. The remaining reluc­
tance of executives toward the busi­
ness budget is probably due to their 
vivid appreciation of the complex fac­
tors entering into business profit and 
their lack of belief in the ability of 
anyone to estimate and measure all 
these various forces. If such execu­
tives could be brought to understand 
that the problem which appears so 
complex and confused to them can 
quite readily and with fair accuracy 
be attacked in reasonable logical se­

quence, the future of the budget as 
the chief instrument of executive con­
trol in business, would be assured.

Judge McCormick, of San Fran­
cisco, says these are the thirteen com­
monest mistakes of life:

1. To attempt to set up your own 
standards of right and wrong.

2. To try to measure the enjoy­
ment of others by your own.

3. To expect uniformity of opin­
ions in this world.

8. To worry ourselves and others 
about what cannot be remedied.

9. To consider a thing impossible 
that we ourselves cannot perform.

10. 
Failing to help everybody, 
wherever, however, and whenever we 
can.

11. To believe only what our in­
finite minds can grasp.

12. 
Not to make allowances for 
the weaknesses of others.

13. To estimate by some outside 
quality when it is that within him 
which makes the man.—Forbes.
From a Bulletin of the Fabricated Production Department of the Chamber

What Is Meant by a Uniform 
Cost System?
By a uniform cost system is meant 
a cost system that will give compar­
able results for a given industry for 
both large and small manufacturers. 
There are enthusiasts who desire a 
uniform cost system that will fit any 
industry, but that is not possible, at 
least at the present time. It is not 
possible because each industry has 
problems and conditions peculiar to 
itself. The shipbuilder has little in 
common with the cotton finisher; the 
stove manufacturer with the pickle 
packer. In a machine industry the 
overhead may be the all-important 
item; in the tanning industry the over­
head is relatively unimportant. In the 
electrical appliance field thousands of 
items may be manufactured; in the 
brick industry just a half dozen sta­
ples. In the dye and packing indus­
tries the accounting for by-products is 
important, while this is a relatively 
unimportant problem for the bottle 
and cement manufacturer, and so 
right down the line.

A uniform cost system will give the 
manufacturer comparable results. 
That is to say, it will give him assur­
ance that the other manufacturers in 
his line have included and excluded 
the same items into costs, that their 
enterprises have been substantially 
departmentalized in the same way as 
his own, that there exists a common 
treatment of overhead, a tying up of 
the financial and cost records, a con­
trol of raw materials. In other words, 
when the manufacturer studies his 
summary of manufacturing cost, he 
will be able to say that A, B, and C 
are figuring their costs in the same 
way he is, and that the difference in 
costs that may exist are based on su-

perior efficiency and not because of 
cost ignorance.

To illustrate what is meant by a 
uniform cost system, let us analyze 
some uniform cost systems actually 
in operation. Note the flexibility and 
their adaptability to accounting condi­
tions in the particular industry.

1. Classification of accounts and 
departmentalization of industry.

4. Standard practice covering the 
treatment of cost of metal and melt­
ing expense and the distribution of 
indirect costs.

6. Standard forms and statements: 
(a) Distribution of fixed plant 
charges, (b) Analysis and distribu­
tion of pattern shop costs, (c) State­
ment of iron costs, (d) Monthly cost 
statement, (e) Statement of metal, 
melting, and overhead rates. (f) 
Casting summary cost record. (g) 
Casting cost record.

Confectionery
In this industry it was considered 
desirable not only to provide for an 
agreement of fundamentals of cost ac­
counting, but to proceed as well with 
the education of members along lines 
of general accounting.

1. Fundamental principles, together 
with a chart showing expense and 
cost factors to be considered in cost 
accounting.

2. Definitions.
3. Departmentalization of business.
4. Classification of expense.

5. Reasons for segregating income 
charges and operating expenses.

6. Overhead classification, applica­
tion into costs and distribution on 
general ledger, together with an over­
head loss and gain statement.

7. Ledger arrangements, financial 
statements, explanation of principal 
accounts.

8. Handling of inventories and col­
lection of cost data.

9. A fairly complete showing of 
simple and effective standardized 
forms, e. g., pay-roll record, compara­
tive cost record, stock cards, material 
reports, product in process journal, 
dipping test, daily report of supplies.

Shipbuilding
The uniform cost system worked 
out for the shipbuilding industry con­
clusively answers those who state that 
a uniform cost system cannot be 
worked out for their particular indus­
try because it is too complicated.

1. Basic divisions of shipyard activ­
ities (departmentalization).

2. Principles and definitions—Ele­
ments of costs together with graphic 
illustrations of plan for collecting, ar­
ranging, and analyzing elements of 
shipyard production costs.

3. Specific definitions — materials, 
labor, and expense.

4. Purposes underlying standard 
method of cost accounts.

5. Production orders — classifica­
tion and methods.

6. Shipyard product units—classi­
fication and methods.

7. Ship works department—classi­
fication and method.

8. Materials and supplies—classifi­
cation and method.

11. Cost methods for production 
control and estimating.

12. Summary cost reports and sta­
tistical statements.

13. Table of rates of depreciation.
14. Classification of asset tools and 
small perishable tools.

A uniform cost system is uniform 
as to fundamentals only. Within its 
general frame work there is wide 
scope and latitude for originality in 
office methods and accommodation to 
particular accounting requirements. 
At the same time a uniform cost sys­
tem is more than a price list or a clas­
sification of accounts or an estimat­
ing of cost sheets.

A truly uniform cost system re­
quires much give and take—the yield­
ing of pet theories—the reconciling 
of divergent practices. There must 
be a wide and thorough interchange 
of cost experiences and cost methods, 
and all elements; both your small and 
large manufacturer must participate 
and co-operate in the devising of it. 
Accordingly, it is a mistake to formu­
late your uniform cost system too early 
in the game. As a correspondent 
wisely puts it, “A ready-made cost 
system hurts worse than a ready-made 
shoe,” and many cost efforts have 
failed because of inadequate prepara­
tion. The interest of the members of 
a trade association must be aroused 
until a point is reached where the 
members will demand the uniform 
cost system rather than have it thrust 
upon them. This interest it is up to 
the secretary to create, which may 
mean much up-hill work.

The following are some advantages 
to be derived from uniform cost ac­
counting which can be brought home 
by letter or through the trade asso­
ciation journal.

1. Strengthens position of indus-

Nothing in this world is per­
fect; some waste of time or money 
or materials occurs in every 
transaction.

To discover the causes of 
waste, to point them out, and to 
contribute in every way possible 
toward reducing them will be the 
constant effort of this depart­
ment.

The above lines appear in a book­
let issued by the Domestic Distribu­
tion Department of the Chamber of 
Commerce of the United States. 
They represent the creed of a power­
ful economic agency in the fight 
against carelessness in industry, which

is costing untold sums in material 
and human effort.

In his book, just published, Henry 
Ford, the automobile pioneer, tells 
how one of the greatest fortunes of 
history was built on the basic prin­
ciple of the elimination of waste. He 
calls waste the unpardonable sin of 
any of the activities of life, resulting 
from lack of understanding of what 
one does, or being careless in the 
doing of it. His organization has 
striven toward manufacture with a 
minimum of waste, distribution at a 
minimum of profit, depending for the 
total profit upon the volume of distri­
bution.

In other lines, too, expansion has 
obtained its real impetus when new 
sources of revenue were opened up 
by finding the ultimate use for a maxi­
mum amount of the raw material. A 
notable instance, is that of the pack­
ers, whose real profits come from 
by-products, manufactured from the 
residue which was thrown away in 
the early days of the industry.

Automobile engineers are striving 
for lower gasoline and oil consump­
tion. Fuel economy is becoming a 
stronger selling point year by year.

Waste caused by improper packing 
and shipping represents a large part 
of the total of freight losses. Rail­
way companies, which bear the brunt 
of the losses, are active in the cam­
paign for better packing, marking, 
and billing to cut down the annual 
claim bill, which in 1921 was recorded 
at $96,750,000.

The value of the accountant and 
systematizer in the reduction and elim­
ination of waste has been clearly 
demonstrated. These pioneers in a 
modern science, business analysis, 
help to find and stop leaks which cut 
into profits.

Burbank Says:
Keep growing like the plants. 
Don’t let weeds grow in your 
mind.

A Good Accounting System
That most of the brokerage firms 
have improved their organizations in 
the last couple of years, says the Wall 
Street Journal, is indicated by the 
snappy way the present bull market 
is being handled by the clerks.

Furthermore, it will be recollected 
that many houses reduced their work­
ing forces considerably as late as the 
fall of 1921, and while some have 
taken on additional employees, this 
has not been the case with all firms.

The systems have been simplified 
to a point where the clerks are able 
to handle a large volume of business 
though it come suddenly.

As one partner of a leading house 
said: “Whereas, such a heavy volume 
of business as we are now enjoying 
would have meant double the number 
of clerks working far into the night 
and all day Saturday and Sunday a 
couple of years ago, we have such a 
good accounting system that by 5 p. 
m. every day our books are up to date 
and we know exactly where we 
stand.”

A shortage of men in all lines of in­
dustry before the middle of Novem­
ber was predicted by Director General 
Francis I. Jones; of the United States 
Employment Service.

“Within the past two months,” he 
says, “employers of labor have been 
coming to this service, asking that 
they be supplied with men, whereas a 
few months ago this service was look­
ing for opportunities to place men at 
work. We are firmly of the opinion

England’s First Payment
Payment of $50,000,000 by Great 
Britain to the United States, the first 
installment of the war debt, last month 
brought with it hope for better condi­
tions in world finance. Chicago Com­
merce has this comment on its prob­
able effect in this country:

Even more than the strengthening of 
sterling exchange, the international pay­
ment should affect stock prices by its 
indication that a further deflation in this 
country will probably not be necessary. 
It means, in effect, that a drastic col­
lapse of values is out of the question and 
that merchants can afford to lay in rea­
sonably full stocks of medium-priced 
merchandise. If the Christmas buying 
season bears out the present indications, 
this increase in the amount of merchan­
dise stock carried, discounting any ad­
vance in business, is enough to account 
for a rise in manufacturing and an up­
ward pull upon every form of active en­
terprise.

Building an Income
Your income is the world’s measure 
of your ability.

Your success in creating with your 
own brain and energy a substantial in­
come compels a certain respect for 
you otherwise withheld. Even those 
dearest to you are influenced in their 
conception of you by your ability to 
provide the many enjoyments and 
comforts resulting from a larger in­
come.

Building an income is therefore the 
most IMPORTANT PRACTICAL 
TASK YOU HAVE.

To build anything the most impor­
tant step is the foundation. If you 
have ever watched the construction of 
a giant skyscraper, story upon story, 
you realize that the foundation 
under such enormous weight must be 
almost flawless; and the higher the 
building, the more solid must be the 
foundation.

Those who enroll for the I. A. S. 
training, plan a foundation on which 
to build a higher personal income than 
would be possible without the course. 
The COMPLETED course is a foun­
dation for hundreds of incomes of 
$5,000, $10,000, and up. It SHOULD 
be the foundation for a much bigger 
income; and it WILL be when com­
pleted.

A knowledge of accounting and 
financial management, which the 
day’s work alone will never give, and 
confidence generated from that 
knowledge—these are practical use- 
able returns from study.

The I. A. S. plan is TWO EXAM­
INATIONS A WEEK. That will 
be building up the FOUNDATION 
upon which rests a bigger income, 
and all that it will bring.

What Is Your Percentage?
Someone said recently that if a man 
sells you his brawn, he sells you 
33 1-3 per cent of himself; if he sells 
you his brawn and his brains, he sells 
66 2-3 per cent; but if he sells you 
his brawn, his brains, and his heart, 
he sells you 100 per cent.

Brawn and brains are purchasable, 
but the heart of a man—his good will, 
his desire to do his best—cannot be 
purchased. It is something that 
comes from an innate conviction of 
the man that his present welfare, his 
future, and his happiness are integral 
parts of his job, and that it devolves 
upon him to take care of all three 
by giving to his job the best that is 
within him.

Have you this conviction ? Can you 
honestly admit to yourself that you 
are giving the best that is within your 
power ? Are you boosting the or­
ganization or are you continually crit­
icising and indulging in petty “knock­
ing” ? Are you more interested in 
what the other fellow is not doing 
than you are in what you are doing? 
If you are doing all that you are sup­
posed to do, there should be very lit­
tle time left to inspect the other fel­
low’s work.

What is your percentage? Take 
stock of yourself. Do it with an hon­
est spirit and do it efficiently.—The 
Kalends.

Alderman Edward Cecil Moore 
was elected Lord Mayor of London 
on September 29. He is a chartered 
accountant by profession and is the 
first member in that field to become 
Lord Mayor.

He thinks it quite a frolic,
To play that game, bucolic, 
And always beats the farmers by a 
very heavy score;
At barnyard golf so clever,
They say that they will never, 
Never toss another shoe, but keep 
them nailed above the door.

So when you feel unhappy, 
Look up this pleasant chap, he 
Will smile away your troubles and 
help you if he can;
Just like he throws a ringer—
He is a good luck bringer, 
For the horseshoe is an omen—and 
a symbol of the man.

Assistant Educational Director, International Accountants Society, Inc.

Readers of the Forum are, of 
course, familiar with the perpetual 
inventory, retail selling price inven­
tory, and other special types of inven­
tories widely used, but probably a very 
much smaller number of these readers 
have had the opportunity of using an 
automatic inventory.

It is unfortunately characteristic of 
many special types of inventory that 
their installation and operation fre­
quently involve considerable additional 
clerical labor and expense, but the au­
tomatic inventory is not subject to this 
criticism.

It is best described by illustrating 
its application in a specific case.

Let us suppose, for example, that 
one of the activities of a business is 
the publication of books or pamphlets. 
An edition of a particular book will 
consist of a definite number of per­
fect copies suitable for sale or use. 
Thus, there may be 1,000 copies of a 
particular edition, and these when in­
spected are placed in stock. When 
these copies are placed in the stock 
room, they are given a consecutive 
number from one to one thousand, 
the number being stamped on a tem­
porary paper cover or wrapper.

As books are taken from stock for 
shipment, the highest number will be 
taken first. Thus if an order calls for 
five books, the requisition for these 
will show that books Nos. 1,000, 999, 
998, 997, and 996 are being withdrawn 
for this particular order, and the ac­
counting department’s copy of the in­
voice will show that these numbers 
were withdrawn. A check list or, in 
other words, a list with numbered 
lines one to one thousand inclusive, 
will be maintained and on this list no­
tation will be made that these num­
bers were withdrawn for a particular 
requisition. This check list will be 
maintained in the stock room, and for

auditing purposes a duplicate may be 
maintained in the accounting depart­
ment, thus giving perpetual inventory 
control in the accounting department 
if desired.

It is quite evident that after we 
have withdrawn from the stock room 
books Nos. 1,000, 999, 998, 997, and 
996, the “top” book remaining in stock 
will carry the number which indicates 
the total number of books of this edi­
tion still on hand. This would be 
995. It is apparent, therefore, that 
all that is necessary in taking stock at 
any time is to make a list of the high­
est numbers in all the editions of the 
publications carried in stock. There 
are at least two sources for this in­
formation. One is the check list 
maintained for each edition of each 
publication. If all requisitions have 
been posted, it would show the high­
est numbers remaining for each pub­
lication. The best information, how­
ever, is based on the actual physical 
count and listing of the “top” books 
in stock.

The basic idea of the automatic in­
ventory is so simple that no extended 
description is required. It is worth 
while, however, to point out that 
wherever large editions are carried, 
the work may be simplified by pack­
ing books in lots of ten, one hundred, 
one thousand, or other convenient lots 
as the particular case may require. 
This would involve the counting of 
the volumes remaining on hand after 
breaking a package, but this would be 
a labor of no consequence.

While the handling of books or 
pamphlets has been illustrated, the 
system is, of course, readily adaptable 
to a great variety of articles.

Trade Associations Approve 
Uniform Cost Accounting
Fifty-two trades associations of 
which the Chamber of Commerce of 
the United States has record have 
applied themselves earnestly to the 
establishing of uniform cost systems.

The principal supporters of uni­
form cost accounting have been the 
trade secretaries, who have devoted 
years to up-hill work, one having 
campaigned for thirteen years before 
winning out. The list, given here, 
omits those associations having only

Uniform Cost Systems 
(Concluded from page 7) 
try in dealing with governmental or 
regulatory bodies.

2. Inspires confidence that selling 
prices are determined upon a fair and 
equitable basis.

3. Solves disputed points of ac­
counting within the industry authori­
tatively.

4. Makes possible a more intelli­
gent competition.

5. Reveals lines within the industry 
which have been marketed on an un­
profitable basis.

6. Shows the danger line below 
which goods cannot be sold at a profit, 
thus serving as an insurer of profits.

7. Acts as a common guide to the 
value, efficiency, and waste of work­
ers, machines, methods, operations, 
and plants.

8. Becomes a reliable guide and 
basis for estimating prospective busi­
ness, thus acting as a forerunner for 
comprehensive production statistics.

9. Furnishes current reports for 
comparing major cost items with 
standards which are predetermined, 
thereby measuring and increasing op­
erating efficiency.

10. Establishes a standard code of 
accounting practice, so that if your 
cost clerk or bookkeeper leaves you, 
his successor will step into a com­
pletely formulated system.

Camp on Production
Walter Camp, health expert and 
football authority, has some interest­
ing views on production. The fol­
lowing remarks appeared in an ar­
ticle contributed to “Collier’s”:

Men who start with the idea that 
in industry production is the prime 
factor are studying very closely the 
relation of the hours of labor to 
productivity. It seems to them un­
necessary to make the hours of labor 
a subject for argument and dispute, 
the occasion, possibly, for costly 
strikes. They are asking whether it 
may not be possible that shorter hours 
or industrial vacations would actually 
increase production without increas­
ing costs.

They don’t know the answer to 
their own question yet. They regard 
it as certain that an individual worker 
can, if he chooses, by speeding and 
intensifying his work, accomplish 
more in eight hours than in nine or 
ten; as possible even that he could do 
more in six hours than in eight. They 
still have a good many doubts as to 
whether men in the mass, employed in 
industry, would actually do anything 
of the sort.

A great deal of exact information 
along this line is being piled up. At 
Port Sunlight, in England, certain ex­
periments, carried out over many 
years, have convinced Lord Lever- 
hulme, one of the most successful 
business men in the world, and one 
who hates and loathes the ideas of 
paternalism and benevolence as ap­
plied to the relations between em­
ployer and worker, that the six-hour 
shift is the one that will most eco­
nomically achieve maximum produc­
tion. The United States Public 
Health Service, in the course of an

exhaustive and, as yet, by no means 
finished research, has published the 
results of a study of two plants, gen­
erally similar, in one of which an 
eight- and in the other a ten-hour 
day prevailed. Its figures indicate 
that the eight-hour plant is the more 
efficient.

A corporation official consented to 
give a caller some figures on produc­
tion. Encountering some difficulty in 
securing the exact data in his own 
files, he enlisted the assistance of the 
accounting department.

The accountants spent a good deal 
of time in verifying the figures before 
consenting to their release. At the 
end of half an hour the caller showed 
signs of unrest. Noticing this, the of­
ficial phoned the accounting depart­
ment and said, “Say, what’s the mat­
ter over there ? Don’t you know 
there’s a man waiting here whose time 
is worth $10 an hour?”

The modest visitor looked up at the 
generous official and said, “Thank 
you for the raise; very kind of you, 
I’m sure.”—Business.

accountants of advanced standing for 
the position of Chief Accountant

To meet the growing demand 
for executive and chief account­
ants this remarkable, practical 
extension course has been pre­
pared. Every duty, oppor­
tunity, and function of the 
Comptroller, Auditor, and 
Chief Accountant is analyzed, 
classified, and clearly set forth 
by thoroughly competent, prac­
tical, experienced accountant­
instructors, with individual at­
tention to the work of each 
student.
Ten lessons in looseleaf form, 
perpetually revised. A num­

ber of original charts and 
graphs give a volume of in­
struction in compact, compre­
hensible, usable form.

To those who are fitted by ex­
perience and training, we will
